coredata.dc:title: Tribochemical mechanism of superlubricity in graphene quantum dots modified DLC films under high contact pressure
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Xuan
Zhang, Jie
Luo, Ting
Cao, Bingqiang
Xu, Jianxun
Chen, Xinchun
Luo, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we designed three series of tribo-couples based on amorphous carbon films including GLC, DLC and PLC that were modified by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The tribo-testing environment was controlled at harsh conditions (like heavy load and high speed) in dry nitrogen atmosphere using bare and film-coated bearing steel balls as counterbodies, respectively. Through the tribochemical interactions, the self-mated DLC system obtained a surperlubricity state (μ = 0.01). During the whole sliding, the contact surface of the upper counterfacing ball was covered by 2D-layered carbon and graphitic lubricants induced via structural transformation of GQDs. Meanwhile, the tribofilm of the disc wear track was composed of a silica-like SiOx boundary layer and a multicomponent mixed-layer induced by tribochemistry. Compared to the self-mated DLC system, the structural boundary enriched with SiOx compounds was not formed at the bottom region of the tribofilm for the bare steel system; meanwhile, the disc wear track was covered by a thicker tribofilm containing plenty of degraded GQDs. This inferred the fact that the formation of a nanostructured sliding interface was the key to realize superlubricity. These discoveries successfully afforded a lubrication mechanism of GQDs for solid lubricant in applications of engineering and industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Superlubricity
Amorphous carbon film
Graphene quantum dots
High contact pressure

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic diagrams of the characterizations. b-d) Friction curves of amorphous carbon films tested under 20 N and dry nitrogen. The rotating speed is 15 cm/s, and the rotating radius is 3 mm. The initial Hertz contact pressure is 1.61 GPa. b) GQDs/GLC films against bare and GLC-coated bearing steel balls, respectively. c) GQDs/DLC films against bare and DLC-coated bearing steel balls, respectively. d) GQDs/PLC films against bare and PLC-coated bearing steel balls, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of contact surfaces of DLC-related tribo-couples after tribo-test under 20 N. a-c) GQDs/DLC film against bare steel ball system, d-f) GQDs/DLC film against DLC-coated bearing steel ball system. a, d) Optical surface morphologies of balls and discs. b, e) 3D white-light interference surface morphologies of balls and discs. c, f) Raman spectra. The green dots on the optical images indicate the measurement positions of Raman. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Analysis of as-formed nanostructured tribofilm for the self-mated DLC system tested under 20 N. a) HRTEM images show the whole cross-sectional morphology of the tribofilm. b) HRTEM images show the bilayer structure of the DLC film from two representative regions (Area I and Area II marked in a)). c) HRTEM images show the whole cross-sectional morphology of the wear track, and two representative parts (Area I and Area II) of the sliding interface. d) HRTEM images highlight bonding structures of the contact surface in Area I of c). The EDS spectra of Area I and Area III in c) are displayed in Fig. 4, and that of Area II in c) are displayed in Fig. S5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

EDS elemental distribution of the as-formed nanostructure on the disc contact surface in self-mated DLC film system tested under 20 N. a, b) Two representative regions of the tribofilm (Area I and Area III as marked in Fig. 3c). The EDS spectra of the cross-sectional interface is acquired on the basis of four representative parts: DLC film, the structural boundary, the bulk of the tribofilm and the FIB metallic protective layer (marked as 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS elemental analysis of the film contact surface in the self-mated GQDs/DLC system before and after the tribo-testing and the corresponding MD simulation of the lubricating mechanism. a-c) C1s, O1s and Si2p XPS spectra, respectively. d-f) The details of the tribo-simulations. d) Preloading process. e) Loading process. f) Unloading process. The survey XPS spectra and their elemental composition are summarized in Fig. S6 and Table S1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Tribochemical mechanism of superlubricity in graphene quantum dots modified DLC films under high contact pressure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Tribology, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Tribology, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Automotive Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Automotive Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Physical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Physical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Physical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, 273165, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we designed three series of tribo-couples based on amorphous carbon films including GLC, DLC and PLC that were modified by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The tribo-testing environment was controlled at harsh conditions (like heavy load and high speed) in dry nitrogen atmosphere using bare and film-coated bearing steel balls as counterbodies, respectively. Through the tribochemical interactions, the self-mated DLC system obtained a surperlubricity state (μ = 0.01). During the whole sliding, the contact surface of the upper counterfacing ball was covered by 2D-layered carbon and graphitic lubricants induced via structural transformation of GQDs. Meanwhile, the tribofilm of the disc wear track was composed of a silica-like SiOx boundary layer and a multicomponent mixed-layer induced by tribochemistry. Compared to the self-mated DLC system, the structural boundary enriched with SiOx compounds was not formed at the bottom region of the tribofilm for the bare steel system; meanwhile, the disc wear track was covered by a thicker tribofilm containing plenty of degraded GQDs. This inferred the fact that the formation of a nanostructured sliding interface was the key to realize superlubricity. These discoveries successfully afforded a lubrication mechanism of GQDs for solid lubricant in applications of engineering and industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, amorphous carbon thin film has already been utilized in engineering and industry, especially in tools, hard disk drives and engines for antifriction and wear resistance. Furthermore, amorphous carbon thin film also has excellent performances such as low surface energy and good biocompatibility, which expand its range of applications beyond tribology [1–3]. The ultralow friction and wear of amorphous carbon films can ensure long durability of machine with negligible energy consumption during rubbing. Amorphous carbon films exhibit significantly different tribological behaviors on the basis of bonding structures and preparation technology. According to the hydrogen concentration incorporated in the carbon matrix, hydrogenated amorphous carbon films have three types: graphite-like carbon (GLC) [4], diamond-like carbon (DLC) [5,6], and polymer-like carbon (PLC) [7,8]. Commonly, hydrogen-containing carbon films and two-dimensional (2D) material films show ultralow friction in the dry inert gaseous environments like nitrogen atmosphere [9–12]. However, ultralow friction and wear cannot be achieved immediately after a short running-in period and is easily disturbed by changes in the surrounding environment. Moreover, the selection of a variety of counterfaces sliding against amorphous carbon film can greatly influence the tribological performances, which has been comprehensively investigated in the past [13,14]. Thereinto, amorphous carbon film sliding against the same type of film material (namely self-mated system) has the superiority to establish a superlubricity state. During the sliding process, the contact surfaces of the upper and lower counterfaces are usually covered by the in-situ formed tribofilm to reduce friction and wear. The growth behaviors and nanostructures of the tribofilms are governed by the interfacial tribochemistry. Weak interactions along the sliding interface are then realized by the presence of low shear-strength lubrication phases such as sp 2-C or the highly-passivated contact surfaces induced by terminating atoms such as hydrogen or fluorine (i.e., van der Waals forces).

With the development of functional coatings, a new type of carbon nanomaterial called carbon quantum dots (QDs) has rapidly attracted great attention from the research community owing to their unique natures such as supersmall size, excellent chemical stability and good biocompatibility [15–17]. Due to the presence of abundant active groups on their supersmall surface, carbon QDs exhibit high aqueous solubility through controlling the functionalized surfaces. On the basis of lubrication theory and nanomaterial technology, carbon QDs have great potential to be utilized as lubricant additives in tribology via self-transformation of spherical nanostructure. Until now, pioneers have already carried out a certain of fundamental work. However, these pioneers found that some carbon QDs were limited in tribological applications, due to the lack of studies in their surface functionalization [18]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as one innovative type of carbon QDs that has 2D-spherical nanostructure, are synthesized from graphene and oxidized graphene (GO). GQDs not only retain the 2D structure, but also exhibit uniquely physicochemical performances [19–21]. GQDs derived from GO are usually kept in solvent to form uniformly stable and active solution with some stabilizers and modifiers such as potassium hydroxide (KOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), dimethyl formamide (DMF) and silane coupling agent for convenient storage and applications [22]. In the previous research [23], we studied a ternary-phase nano-system based on GQDs. We found that GQDs and nanodiamonds could roll into the bulk structures of polytetrafluoroethylene and MXenes, which intercalated with each other to reduce friction and wear in the ambient environment. However, the role of GQDs in affecting the friction and wear during sliding and their synergetic lubrication behaviors with other nanomaterials are unclear, which requires to be thoroughly explored. Besides, the relationship between GQDs and GO in tribology is also extreme scarcity. It is unclear about the structural transformation between them during sliding. Meanwhile, the lubrication mechanism of GQDs acting as solid lubricant is also blank [24,25]. Therefore, there is a need to design a single system between friction pairs and GQDs such as self-mated tribo-couples based on amorphous carbon system and steel/amorphous carbon system in the inert atmosphere, which is beneficial for investigating the structure-related transformation and tribological mechanism of GQDs.

In this work, we designed three series of lubrication systems based on GLC, DLC and PLC, which were synthesized by an ion vapor deposition system [13]. Each system was paired with two types of counterfaces, namely bare and film-coated bearing steel balls (self-mated ones). All the amorphous carbon films deposited on silicon substrates were surface-modified by GQDs coating under ambient environment. Additionally, the underlying mechanisms regarding the improved lubricities of three lubrication systems and their broadened adaptability in the harsh environments (heavy load and high rotating speed) and inert gaseous atmosphere (dry nitrogen) are investigated with respect to the lubricity of GQDs. The new findings afford a more efficient design of superlubricious systems based on carbon dots induced-construction of the sliding interface and explore a promising perspective in engineering applications under complex working environments.

Amorphous carbon films, including graphite-like carbon (GLC), diamond-like carbon (DLC), polymer-like carbon (PLC), were synthesized by an ion vapor deposition system. The set-up of this coater and the relevant details about deposition procedure had been reported elsewhere [13]. The composition and structure of the films were tailored by introducing different gas sources such as toluene and by controlling the bias voltages. The synthesis procedures of GLC, DLC and PLC are as follows: (1) silicon substrates were ultrasonically cleaned to remove the surface contaminants, (2) the vacuum chamber was pumped to a base pressure of ∼3.0 × 10−3 Pa at the temperature of 200 °C, (3) Ar+ ion bombarding was implemented for 30 min before deposition, (4) an interlayer of a-C:H:Si with a thickness of 400 nm was first deposited using a gas mixture of toluene and tetramethylsilane to improve the adhesion between the top layer and the substrate, and (5) the top a-C:H film was then deposited using the gas of toluene. The hydrogen content of the film was controlled by the pulsed bias voltage, in which 4.5, 2 and 0.25 kV were used for GLC, DLC and PLC, respectively. The total thickness of the samples was kept constant at around 1.4 μm. The films’ hardness was measured by a nanoindentation equipment (NHT2, Anton Paar) using an indentation load of 1 mN, which produced an indentation depth below 1/10 of the film thickness. The as-recorded hardness for the above three kinds of films was 16.5, 20 and 8 GPa, respectively. The GQDs powders were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich LLC, which were of spherical shapes with average dot diameter smaller than 5 nm. Besides carbon, the pristine GQDs also possessed many active elements such as Si, O, Cl, Ca and Na, resulting from the surface modification by some specific surfactants during the synthesis of chemical solution method.

For the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified amorphous carbon film, anhydrous ethanol was used as solvent to prepare mixed solution. Anhydrous ethanol was first poured into a glass beaker, and GQDs powders were added to the glass beaker. The mass concentration in solvent was 1 mg/mL. Then the glass beaker was sealed at room temperature and subjected to ultrasonic oscillation for 10 min. Afterwards, the uniformly mixed solution was slowly added to cover the whole surface of the three types of amorphous carbon films (including GLC, DLC and PLC films). And these samples were stored at room temperature until the solution was volatilized completely, leaving GQDs being adsorbed on the amorphous carbon film surface. It should be pointed out that the structure of the component was finally retained due to the physical nature of the whole preparation process.

The tribo-test was conducted using a TRB3 pin-on-disc tribometer (Anton Paar) at room temperature. The amorphous carbon films (including GLC, DLC and PLC films) coated on Si wafers were clamped on a rotating sample platform, meanwhile employing bare and film-coated bearing steel balls as counterparts, respectively. In this research, the applied normal load was controlled at 20 N, and the testing environment is dry nitrogen atmosphere. It should be pointed out that the nitrogen atmosphere in this work was achieved by purging the nitrogen gas into the sealed chamber of the tribometer. The air was expelled from an outlet hole. Using this method, it was speculated that it was almost impossible to exhaust all the remaining moisture in the chamber. Therefore, it was a ‘humid’ nitrogen atmosphere, which might cause different friction behaviors in contrast to the previous N2-mediated experiments [13]. In those tests, the nitrogen gas was directly purging to the border of the contact area, which assure a completely dry and inert atmosphere. A bearing steel ball with a diameter of 6 mm was used as the counterfacing ball. The rotating speed was 15 cm/s, and the sliding radius was set at 3 mm. The applied normal load was 20 N. The Young’s moduli of bearing steel and silicon were 210 and 140 GPa, respectively. Assuming their Poisson’s ratios to be 0.3 and 0.28, separately, the initial maximum Hertz contact pressure was calculated to be 1.61 GPa. All tribo-tests were repeated at least 5 times to ensure the reproducibility of recorded results. The optical microscope (Nikon optical microscopy) and three-dimensional (3D) white-light interference topography (ZYGO NewView™ 8000) were utilized to characterize the morphology of the targeted surfaces before and after tribo-testing. Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800) was performed with Ar+ laser wavelength of 514.5 nm. To reveal the structural changing, the chemical properties of GQDs/DLC films were monitored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantera II). The acquisition was performed using a monochromatic Al Kα X-rays source with energy of 1486.6 eV. The applied power and beam diameter of X-rays were 23.56 W and 100.0 μm, respectively. The angle of incidence was set at 45 deg for X-rays. The preparation of lamellar samples was conducted by focused ion beam/scanning electron microscope (SEM/FIB, FEI Quanta 3D FEG) dual-beam system based on site- and material-specific lift-out technique. The system was capable of low-kV Ga+ ion milling, meanwhile superior SEM images are delivered for positioning. Before FIB-milling, the contact areas were first coated by a protective Cr layer in an ion sputtering system. Then, these samples were transferred into the SEM/FIB chamber. Afterwards, a thick Pt supporting layer was deposited on the Cr layer before the milling process. During the whole FIB-milling process, careful adjustments of parameters (e.g., ion beam voltage, beam current, probe size) were controlled to protect the surface integrity and the features of interest, especially the sliding contact interface without structural damage or implantation artefacts. The thickness of the lamellar samples was below 100 nm, which was beneficial for analyzing the cross-sectional structures of the tribofilm by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL 2010F) operating at 200 kV. Elemental mappings were detected via EDS equipped in the TEM facility.

A summary of the tribological methodology and performances of amorphous carbon films tested under dry nitrogen is shown in Fig. 1 . In Fig. 1(a), the schematic shows the technological principles in this work. The testing condition is under room temperature of 20 °C in the dry nitrogen atmosphere. A heavy load of 20 N, corresponding to an initial Hertz contact pressure of 1.61 GPa, is used for the tribo-couples. Meanwhile, the characterizations of sliding surface and interface are shown in detail. Fig. 1(b) displays the friction coefficients (μ) of GQDs/GLC films against bare and GLC-coated bearing steel balls, respectively. In the bare steel system, the μ maintains 0.026 after a running-in period (14.2 m). This value holds for 197 s and then quickly increases, which indicates that the GLC film has been worn out. The slight fluctuation of the friction curves illustrates a relatively strong rubbing interaction between GLC film disc and bare bearing steel ball induced by the presence of GQDs. To further understand the effect of GQDs on the GLC film, the bare bearing steel ball is changed to GLC-coated bearing steel ball. After a short running-in period (1.81 m), the μ arrives in an average of about 0.025 ± 0.0051, and then it continues to fall to 0.017 ± 0.0045 at 46.0 m, by which the film is not worn out. When the coated film material is replaced by DLC, the friction curves exhibit some new changes. The μ of GQDs/DLC film against bare bearing steel ball has almost the same behaviour like GQDs/GLC film after the running-in period except the shorter duration time (125 s), as shown in Fig. 1(c). When the counterparts are chosen as DLC-coated balls, the steady-state μ is 0.010 ± 0.0019, which is within the scope of superlubricity (μ ≤ 0.01). The value of self-mated counterparts is much lower than the DLC vs steel tribocouples. In comparison, the steady-state μ in PLC/bare steel system is about 0.027 ± 0.0034 after the running-in period (6.79 m), and comes through an abrupt increase at 135 s (Fig. 1(d)). For the self-mated tribo-couple of GQDs/PLC film against PLC-coated ball, however, almost without an obvious running-in period, the μ quickly arrives at a steady state and achieves a value of 0.014 ± 0.0052, which is also not worn out. Additional friction data is shown in Fig. S0 for reference. For instance, the μ of the unmodified tribocouples of self-mated DLC, self-mated PLC and self-mated GLC films are 0.066, 0.032 and 0.048, respectively. As mentioned above, in this work the remaining moisture in nitrogen atmosphere might significantly affect the friction performances of carbon films. For instance, as reported by many literatures, the hydrogen-rich PLC film is very sensitive to the environmental humidity and losing their superlubricity capacity. Irrespective of the different loads used in these studies, the present μ (0.014) is much higher than that observed in dry nitrogen (i.e., μ < 0.01) [13]. Commonly, the tribological performances of some amorphous carbon films are not satisfied for applications if the surface is unmodified by 2D materials or other low-dimensional lubricants. For instance, hydrogen-free or hydrogen-deficient amorphous carbon (such as GLC and DLC) films are usually confronted with a high friction coefficient larger than 0.1 when tribo-tested in dry inert atmosphere or vacuum environment. In this research, the novel design of GQDs/amorphous carbon film provides a potential high-performance lubricity, especially in the DLC film. However, it should be pointed out that the durability of superlubricity in the self-mated DLC system is very short under the heavy load, which is speculated to be the removal of the as-formed tribofilm and hence the exposure of the underlying hydrogen-deficient DLC surfaces. It is well known that hydrogen-deficient DLC film cannot provide effective lubricity in dry inert gaseous atmosphere such as N2. Even though, the present result has vital significance to reveal the antifriction mechanism of GQDs coating.

To thoroughly comprehend the effects of GQDs on tribological performances and morphologies, the contact surfaces of the DLC-related tribo-couples are characterized by optical microscope, 3D white-light interference and Raman (Fig. 2 ). It is obviously found that the ball scar is covered by plentiful black wear debris (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). Compared with the Raman spectra of bare steel ball, the ball scar is mainly covered by carbon due to the appearance of D-peak (1333 cm−1) and G-peak (1576 cm−1), which are assigned to graphitic carbon [26,27]. And the degree of graphitization is estimated by calculating the ratio of D-peak and G-peak intensities (namely I
D /I
G ) [28]. The transferred film formed on the bare bearing steel ball with characteristic graphitic carbon signals of Raman spectra confirm the occurrence of sliding-induced graphitization during sliding: GQDs are partly degraded, and the residual GQDs are transferred to the surface of the bearing steel ball. Besides, the as-fitted peak ratio of I
D /I
G is 0.76 ± 0.01, which is lower than that of GO (0.83 ± 0.01 [29]) and GQDs (1.29 ± 0.02). The result indicates that GQDs are playing a critical role during the lubrication process based on the mechanism of graphitization [28]. Meanwhile, it is also reasonable to infer that the surface of the bare steel ball is covered by more local-clustered carbon phases during rubbing in view of the enhanced D-peak (Fig. 2(c)), which is similar to the structural evolution of GO [30]. On the basis of similar results observed by Raman spectra, it is believed that the structural transformations of GQDs and GO are quite similar during sliding. From the observed results of optical and 3D white-light interference, it seems that the disc contact surface is also covered by some wear debris. The Raman spectrum of the wear track is similar with that of the pristine DLC-disc, in which the I
D /I
G ratio (0.57 ± 0.01) of the disc track is slightly lower than that (0.58 ± 0.01) of the pristine DLC-disc. The most of GQDs are transferred onto the surface of the steel ball and form far more sp 2-C phases therein. However, the morphological images and Raman spectra are more distinguished in the self-mated system of GQDs/DLC against DLC-coated bearing steel ball. Firstly, the surfaces of the ball scar and disc track are much cleaner and less worn (Fig. 2(d) and (e)). Secondly, the I
D /I
G ratio (0.63 ± 0.01) of the disc track is equal to that (0.63 ± 0.01) of the ball scar, which are higher than those of the pristine DLC-ball (0.58 ± 0.01) and pristine DLC-disc (0.58 ± 0.01). Even though the difference in I
D /I
G ratio is not significant, it can still provide some indications regarding the structural evolution of these lubricants after friction, especially the sliding-induced tribo-products. The increased I
D /I
G ratio proves the increase of local-clustered carbon phase after the introduction of GQDs (Fig. 2(f)). In addition, the wear resistances of the tribo-couples were evaluated by the calculated specific wear rates. By measuring the cross-sectional morphologies of the wear tracks (Fig. 2(b) and (e)), the wear rates of film surfaces in the GQDs/DLC against bare steel ball and self-mated DLC system are 1.35 × 10−6 mm3/Nm and 2.83 × 10−6 mm3/Nm, respectively.

These results infer that more graphitic carbon is formed on the contact surface by interfacial sliding, which improves the tribological performances. Compared to the bare steel system, the newly formed graphitic carbon effectively reinforces the lubricity to attain superlubricity. The formation of carbonaceous tribofilm on the ball surface is consistent with the low μ and the slight wear during sliding. And the graphitization of DLC films is usually apt to occur during sliding and strongly linked to their tribological performances. Commonly, carbon-based lubricants such as graphene and modified graphene nanoflakes and powders are not capable to bear a very high contact pressure such as 1.0 GPa [14]. However, in the present research, the initial peak contact pressure is at least one and a half times larger than this value. The high contact pressure does not have an influence in efforts of GQDs on decreasing friction and wear during the tribo-testing, though the durability of superlubricity is only 100 s (Fig. 1(c)). As shown by Figs. S1 and S2, the morphology images and Raman spectra also indicate the similar results. To further explore these efforts of GQDs, the counterfaces are sliced by focused ion beam (FIB) to obtain lamellar samples (Fig. 1(a) and S3) and to analyze the structural variation on the contact interface by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM).

Fig. 3 displays the as-formed nanostructured tribofilm on the counterfaces in the self-mated DLC system. First of all, the lamellar sample of the upper counterfacing ball is analyzed in detail (Fig. 3(a)). The bulk film coated on the ball scar can be divided into three representative portions: top-most tribofilm, middle sublayer (Area I), and underlying interlayer (Area II). The enlarged image of the top-most region shows a type of unique phenomenon: a narrow shear band (the width is 15 nm) formed below the protective Cr layer. It is speculated as the tribofilm formed during the sliding process. As shown by the HRTEM images, a boundary enriched with nanoflakes (the width is 2 nm) is located at the bottom of the tribofilm. The lamellar spacing of the nanoflake is 0.41 nm, which is close to that of 2D-layered graphene (0.35 nm). According to the microstructure of the tribofilm on the contact surface, the discovery verifies the fact that partial GQDs are transferred to form 2D-layered carbon. Meanwhile, above the nanoflake-rich boundary, the residual GQDs are transformed into graphitic-like carbon. Similarly, the tribofilm of the ball scar in the bare steel system is also found to contain some intact GQDs; meanwhile, a mass of degraded GQDs form graphitic carbon to prevent the transformation of DLC (Fig. S3). Below the tribofilm (Fig. 3(b)), the middle sublayer (Area I) and underlying interlayer (Area II) mainly consist of amorphous carbon (here is DLC film), which implies that the underlying DLC sublayers are not tribo-affected. As the paired counterface, the microstructure of the tribofilm formed on the disc wear track is another important point to reveal the lubrication mechanism of GQDs and the tribo-transformation similarity between GQDs and GO. The lamellar sample of the paired disc wear surface is therefore analyzed in detail.

The tribo-interface of the disc wear track can also be divided into two representative portions: straight (Area I) and wavy regions (Area II and Area III), which are shown in Fig. 3(c). Interestingly, the Area I displays a peculiar interface that has a boundary at the bottom of the tribofilm. Below this boundary is the DLC sublayer. Similar to Area I, the Area II and Area III also have structural boundaries at the bottom of the tribofilm. The newly-formed boundary consists of transferred GQDs, which probably has various elements. The types of these elements are far more than carbon and hydrogen derived from DLC film. Unlike graphene or GO, GQDs are usually modified by active elements such as Si, O, Cl, Ca and Na from surfactants for convenient and efficient applications. Together with GQDs, these functional groups are potentially involved in forming a novel structure (namely a tribofilm), which is favorable to enhance the overall lubricity of GQDs during sliding (Fig. 3(d)). In comparison, for the bare steel system (Fig. S3), there is no such a kind of structural boundary formed at the bottom of the tribofilm on disc side; besides, plenty of degraded GQDs are distributed to cover the disc contact surface of DLC film and form a thicker tribofilm. To clarify the constitution of the tribofilm, the structural boundary and degraded GQDs will be systematically investigated by X-ray energy dispersive spectra (EDS).

The straight (Area I) and wavy regions (Area III) are selected for EDS elemental analysis. The cross-sectional interface is analyzed on the basis of four representative parts in detail: from DLC film, to the structural boundary, to the bulk of the, tribofilm, and finally to the FIB metallic protective layer (marked as 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively). In Fig. 4 (a), the EDS spectra distinctly display the constitution of the structural boundary. It mainly consists of Si (red image) and O (green image) elements (marked as 2), which are the basis to form a silica-like structure. These results also verify the speculation obtained by HRTEM (Fig. 3(c)) that this boundary is the initial growth sublayer for the tribofilm. And the DLC film (marked as 1) is mostly composed of C element (yellow color), which is consistent with the observation as found in Fig. 3(c). Furthermore, the bulk of the tribofilm (marked as 3) is mainly composed of O, C and Si elements, especially a strong intensity of O element. This infers that various covalent compounds such as silica-like SiOx and carbon-based phases are formed in the tribofilm during sliding. As shown by Fig. 4(b), a similar finding is also observed for Area II. Besides, Ca element is found in the tribofilm (i.e., particle-like structure at the sliding interface). This is probably due to the fact that GQDs are active to form a novel structure in the tribofilm. For another representative position of Area II, Fig. S4 also displays the similar results. However, the tribofilm in this region contains much more elements such as Na, K and Cl (Fig. S4(c)). Comparatively, in Fig. S5, no such a structural boundary exits in the tribofilm for the bare steel system; meanwhile, the Si element is too less to be detected by EDS. These results indicate that the formation of the Si–O silica-like sublayer and a carbon-based tribolayer are the key to realize superlubricity. On the basis of the above EDS results, it is reasonable to believe that tribochemistry is taking place on the contact surface during sliding. From Figs. 3 and 4, the tribofilm formed on the DLC surface consists of two sublayers: Si–O boundary sublayer and multicomponent top layer.

To comprehensively understand the lubrication mechanism of GQDs, the constitution of the tribochemical boundary especially the formation of silica-like SiOx and related covalent bonds on the sliding contact surface is analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The relevant results of the chemical elements and bonding states of the disc wear track are listed in Fig. 5 and S6. Fig. S6 displays the XPS survey spectra of the pristine GQDs and the disc wear track of the self-mated DLC system. The elemental compositions are summarized in Tables S1 and S2. Obviously, GQDs have many elements such as Si, O, Cl, Ca and Na, which directly verifies the fact that the multicomponent of the tribofilm is derived from GQDs rather than from the experimental environment. However, after the tribo-testing, the contact surface only contains Si, C and O elements. The other trace elements such as Cl, Ca and Na are too less to be detected by XPS.

Based on the above findings, Si, O and C elements are selected to analyze the chemical characteristics of the tribofilm by XPS peak fitting. In Fig. 5 (a), the pristine surface contains CC (284.5 eV), C–C (285.3 eV), C–O (287.8 eV) and CO (289.3 eV) covalent bonds before sliding. However, the surface groups of CO are converted to C–O after sliding. Meanwhile, a higher proportion of CC (285.4 eV) is achieved, which indicates that more sp2-bonded phases are produced from GQDs or transformed from C–C (sp3-C bond) structure of DLC [31]. Besides, these results also verify the fact that the increase of ID/IG ratio is caused by the increase of local-clustered carbon. Consequently, the carbon concentration is gradually enhanced via graphitization during sliding [24]. In Fig. 5(b), before sliding, O1s has three main chemical peaks: 529.5 eV, 530.3 eV, 533.8 eV, which are ascribed to three kinds of oxygen-related bonds (Si–O, CO and C–O). While, after sliding, they evolved into two main peaks located at 531.2 eV and 534.1 eV, respectively. The intensity of Si–O is reinforced distinctly, which is induced by chemical reactions [32]. These results indicate that the DLC film surface is oxidized by GQDs and simultaneously graphitized during sliding, which is also consistent with the Raman results. Fig. 5(c) displays the bonding variation of Si2p before and after sliding. The positions of three peaks before (after) the friction tests are located at about 101.1 eV (101.4 eV), 101.6 eV (101.9 eV) and 102.9 eV (102.9 eV), corresponding to Si–O p1/2, Si–O p3/2 and C–O–Si, respectively, which indicate small chemical shift during sliding. Meanwhile, all FWHMs of the three peaks decrease because of the decrease of oxygen amount induced via chemical reactions (Fig. S6 and Table S1). The evolution of C–O–Si confirms the formation of Si–O and C–O covalent bonds on the sliding contact surface [33]. Meanwhile, the XPS spectra also confirm the increase of element ratios such as carbon/oxygen, carbon/silicon and silicon/oxygen of the wear track in the self-mated DLC system during sliding (Tables S1 and S2). These changes indicate that the contact surface is involved in the continuous formation and breakage of covalent bonds by tribochemistry. Consequently, wear of self-mated DLC system is effectively inhibited by the synergetic effect from the silica-like SiOx boundary layer and multicomponent top layer.

Generally, the lubrication mechanism of nanomaterials is based on the interfacial activities that the formation of nanostructured lubricants on the rubbing surfaces can inhibit the direct contact between them. The nanolubricants can form a lubricative tribofilm on the sliding surfaces where the contact asperities are separated by interfacial physical or chemical adsorption. Meanwhile, GQDs are probably acting as nanospheres to accelerate rolling effect under external load, which induces synergetic lubricity with amorphous carbon [34]. In this research, the lubrication mechanism is detailly recommended as follows: (1) GQDs are transferred onto the counterfacing surfaces to form tribofilm after a short running-in period; (2) the tribofilm formed on the ball wear scar mainly consists of amorphous carbon with nanoflakes buried in the matrix, and that of the disc wear track is composed of a silica-like SiOx sublayer and multicomponent top layer induced through tribochemical reactions; (3) Ultralow friction is enabled in the presence of these nanostructures across the sliding interface. This proposal is also supported by the molecular dynamics simulations as shown in Fig. 5(d)–(f). During the preloading process, GQDs containing multifunctional groups are dispersed uniformly on the surface of the pristine DLC-disc. After loading, the tribofilm is formed on the contact surface via interfacial sliding-induced tribochemistry. Finally, the surface of the DLC-ball is covered by a 2D-layered carbon sublayer and a multicomponent mixed-layer; meanwhile, the surface of the DLC-disc is covered by the Si–O enrich-layer and mixed-layer. Numerous local-clustered carbon nanostructures are distributed along the sliding interface (mixed-layer) and the superlubricity is a result of the synergetic effect from all these nanostructured and multicomponent lubricants.

The present research provides chemical and microscopic insights into the unique lubricity of GQDs, which potentially improve the tribological performances of various tribo-couple materials. And this modification is generally realized by the formation of carbon-based tribofilm with active interfacial nanostructures. The structural transformation during rubbing are very similar for GQDs and GO. Moreover, the interfacial nanostructure and bonding state of the tribofilm are highly influenced by the counterpart materials. For the self-mated contacts of amorphous carbon, GQDs undergo the structural degradation to form an amorphous carbon-based tribofilm during sliding. The direct spreading of GQDs on the contact surface is favorable to form a silica-like SiOx enrich-layer and a multicomponent mixed-layer through tribochemical reactions, which are beneficial for achieving superlow friction. These discoveries possibly afford a practical guidance to design GQDs-based or even QDs-based lubricants, and broaden the applications of supersmall nanomaterials upon engineering and industry.
The authors declare that all data supporting the finding of this study are available within the paper and its supplementary information.

Xuan Yin: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jie Zhang: Investigation. Ting Luo: Investigation. Bingqiang Cao: Resources, Validation. Jianxun Xu: Resources, Validation. Xinchun Chen: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Jianbin Luo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
